title: 攻破 LLM 驱动的应用：从上下文投毒、工具越权到纵深防御
author: Spring I/O
content:
Breaching LLM-Powered Applications

Brian Vermeer’s central warning is that adding an LLM does not replace ordinary application security. It adds a nondeterministic decision-maker on top of familiar code, data stores, authorization rules, retrieved documents, conversation history, and executable tools. A safe architecture must therefore protect the entire context and every side effect, not merely polish the prompt.

The expanded attack surface

An LLM application sends more than a user message. The request can include a system instruction, prior messages presented as memory, content retrieved through RAG, and definitions for functions or MCP tools. Each source can influence the response. Once the model can call tools, a manipulated context can become an actual database update, file operation, booking cancellation, or data disclosure.

RAG poisoning through a conventional bug

The first car-rental demonstration starts with an unsafe Spring Boot file-upload endpoint that trusts the original filename. A path-traversal payload lets an attacker overwrite the application’s terms-of-use document. When the document is re-chunked into the vector store, a fabricated exception becomes part of the retrieved context. The assistant then accepts the attacker’s secret phrase, cancels a booking that policy should reject, and follows the poisoned instructions.

The lesson is broader than the specific demo: retrieved content is an input boundary. Documents, ingestion pipelines, storage permissions, chunking jobs, and provenance controls all affect what the model will later treat as evidence.

Chat-memory poisoning and vulnerability chaining

Chat memory is not memory inside the model; it is conversation history repeatedly attached to later requests. In the second demonstration, SQL injection inserts a fake exchange into stored history. The fabricated assistant messages claim that the user is allowed to cancel at any time. A later request replays that context, and the model acts as though it has already made a binding promise.

This chains a decades-old vulnerability with a new execution layer. Path traversal, SQL injection, cross-site scripting, vulnerable packages, and broken access control remain dangerous, and an LLM can amplify their consequences.

Prompt injection and excessive authority

Direct instructions such as dropping database tables become catastrophic when the application exposes a generic SQL execution function. A model may report failure even when the tool call succeeded, so the natural-language response is not a trustworthy audit record.

Stronger models may resist a blunt request for all user information, yet a user can split the request into smaller questions—counts, first names, last names, and addresses—and ask the assistant to combine the accumulated answers. Individually harmless-looking prompts can reconstruct protected data through shared context.

Guardrails help, but they are not the security model

The demo adds an input guard that classifies malicious requests before they reach the main model and its tools. Similar controls can sanitize outputs. Vermeer recommends layering inexpensive deterministic checks first and more costly model-based checks later. Guardrails reduce risk, but they are probabilistic and should complement—not replace—authorization, validation, and narrow interfaces.

Design tools for least privilege

Tool functions should be small, explicit, and bound to the current user’s permissions. A chatbot should not receive a broad create-delete-update capability merely because one workflow needs a read operation. High-risk actions should require human confirmation and pass through normal authentication and authorization paths.

The local-model example shows another problem: when required parameters are missing, a model may invent an email address, telephone number, password, or street address and pass those guesses to a real function. Structured inputs, validation, confirmation, and capability-specific tools prevent fuzzy language reasoning from silently becoming corrupted state.

Keep deterministic workflows deterministic

Not every feature needs a chat interface. Critical state transitions should remain programmatic so a model cannot jump from one lifecycle state to another and bypass required checks. Structured input and output also reduce the freedom to produce unexpected formats or actions.

Observability is essential because model behavior is nondeterministic. Record the prompt context that is safe to retain, selected tools, validated arguments, authorization decisions, actual side effects, and failures. When something goes wrong, teams need evidence of what executed—not only what the assistant said.

Privacy, routing, and cost controls

Sending corporate or personal data to a hosted model can create privacy and regulatory exposure. The talk recommends splitting the system into smaller services and routing sensitive workloads to local, on-premises, or private-cloud models. Each model-facing service should receive only the data and functions needed for its task.

Input validation also protects cost. An unrestricted support chatbot can be used for unrelated explanations, poems, or other token-burning requests that generate no product value.

A practical security checklist

  • Continue scanning for conventional vulnerabilities and unsafe dependencies.
  • Treat prompts, retrieved documents, chat history, and tool descriptions as untrusted inputs.
  • Sanitize both model input and output.
  • Write strict system instructions, while assuming they can still be bypassed.
  • Enforce authorization and access control in application code.
  • Bind narrow tools to narrow services and current user privileges.
  • Require a human in the loop for destructive or high-impact operations.
  • Use structured schemas when free-form text is unnecessary.
  • Choose models according to task, security, privacy, and deployment needs.
  • Audit third-party MCP servers and downloaded agent skills before granting them access.

The final warning extends beyond deployed chatbots. Agent skills and MCP servers downloaded from the internet can carry prompt injections, suspicious network behavior, or code that operates with powerful local credentials. They deserve the same review, sandboxing, least-privilege access, and observability as any other dependency.
aiSummary: 这场安全实战演讲展示了传统应用漏洞如何污染 LLM 上下文并触发危险的工具操作，进而提出最小权限、授权校验、护栏、可观测性与人工控制等具体防护实践。
oneSentenceSummary: 这场安全实战演讲展示了传统应用漏洞如何污染 LLM 上下文并触发危险的工具操作，进而提出最小权限、授权校验、护栏、可观测性与人工控制等具体防护实践。
summary: Brian Vermeer 通过一个刻意保留漏洞的 Spring Boot 租车应用，说明 LLM 系统不仅会继承传统软件的弱点，还在提示词、RAG 文档、聊天历史、模型与工具之间引入了新的信任边界。现场攻击演示包括：利用路径遍历篡改检索到的政策内容，通过 SQL 注入伪造记忆，使用多步提示绕过信息披露规则，借助权限过大的工具执行破坏性 SQL，以及诱使较小的模型虚构敏感账户参数。演讲随后构建了一套纵深防御架构：保障依赖项与传统代码安全，将所有上下文来源视为不可信输入，分层部署输入和输出护栏，在模型之外执行授权校验，仅暴露能力明确且范围狭窄的工具，高风险操作必须经过确认，维持确定性的状态转换，观测真实发生的副作用，将敏感工作负载路由到私有模型，并审计 MCP 服务器与下载的智能体技能。

mainPoints.point: 整个 LLM 上下文都是攻击面
mainPoints.explanation: 系统指令、检索文档、聊天历史、用户提示和工具定义都会影响模型行为，因此只保护用户可见的提示词，会让多个可被利用的信任边界处于无防护状态。

mainPoints.point: 传统漏洞可能演变为 LLM 执行攻击链
mainPoints.explanation: 演示将路径遍历与 RAG 投毒相连，也将 SQL 注入与伪造聊天记忆相连，说明常见漏洞能够操纵模型上下文，并进一步升级为未经授权的现实操作。

mainPoints.point: 工具安全必须由应用代码强制保障
mainPoints.explanation: 通用 SQL 或 CRUD 工具会赋予非确定性模型过大的权限。通过范围狭窄的函数、绑定用户身份的授权校验、参数验证、结构化 schema 与明确确认机制，可以把安全决策置于自然语言推理之外。

mainPoints.point: 护栏可以降低风险，但不能替代安全控制
mainPoints.explanation: 输入与输出检查可以结合低成本的确定性规则和专用模型分类器，但它们仍可能失效，必须与访问控制、安全编码、最小权限和确定性工作流共同使用。

mainPoints.point: 可观测性、隐私路由与依赖审查是架构级要求
mainPoints.explanation: 团队需要记录实际的工具调用及其副作用，将受监管数据路由到隐私保障适当的模型，并在授予凭据或本地系统访问权限之前审查 MCP 服务器和智能体技能。

keyQuotes: 你检查过 MCP 的代码吗？
因为现在由我掌控，而不是 LLM。
据我所知，希望驱动开发并不存在。
为不同任务选择合适的 LLM。
